title: OpenAI Releases GPT-5.5, Its Most Capable Agentic Model Yet
summary: OpenAI launched GPT-5.5, a ground-up retrained model designed to handle complex, multi-step tasks autonomously with state-of-the-art coding, research, and computer-use capabilities.
content: OpenAI released GPT-5.5 on April 23, 2026, calling it their 'smartest and most intuitive to use model yet' and the next step toward a new way of getting work done on a computer. The model excels at writing and debugging code, researching online, analyzing data, and operating software autonomously across tools — users can hand it a messy, multi-part task and trust it to plan, iterate, and keep going without constant supervision. GPT-5.5 achieves 82.7% on Terminal-Bench 2.0 and 58.6% on SWE-Bench Pro, outperforming Claude Opus 4.7 and Gemini 3.1 Pro on most coding benchmarks, while matching GPT-5.4's per-token latency and using significantly fewer tokens. An internal version of GPT-5.5 helped discover a new mathematical proof about Ramsey numbers, and a researcher used it to analyze a 62-sample, 28,000-gene dataset in a session that would have taken his team months. The model rolls out to Plus, Pro, Business, and Enterprise ChatGPT and Codex users immediately, with API access priced at $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens.

title: Google Overhauled Enterprise AI Strategy with Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform
summary: Google rebranded Vertex AI into the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, a unified system for building, deploying, governing, and monitoring AI agents at scale across large organizations.
content: At Google Cloud Next in Las Vegas, Google unveiled the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, a full overhaul of its enterprise AI offering built to address the challenge of managing hundreds or thousands of AI agents at once. The platform is organized around four pillars — build, scale, govern, and optimize — and includes Agent Studio (a low-code interface for creating agents), an Agent Development Kit with graph-based multi-agent orchestration, and a marketplace of pre-built agents from Atlassian, Oracle, ServiceNow, and Workday. Critically, governance tools include Agent Identity (cryptographic IDs for every agent), Agent Gateway (policy enforcement against prompt injection and data leakage), and Agent Anomaly Detection to flag suspicious behavior before agents go rogue. Google CEO Sundar Pichai noted that 75% of all code at Google is now AI-generated and approved by engineers, and that a recent internal code migration completed by agents ran six times faster than was possible a year ago. Google also announced a $750 million fund to support its partner ecosystem and agreements with McKinsey, Deloitte, and other consultants for early access to upcoming Google DeepMind models.

title: OpenAI Releases Privacy Filter, an Open-Weight PII Detection Model
summary: OpenAI open-sourced Privacy Filter, a 1.5B-parameter model that detects and redacts personally identifiable information in text locally without sending data to a server.
content: OpenAI released Privacy Filter on April 22, 2026, an open-weight model under the Apache 2.0 license designed to detect and redact personally identifiable information (PII) in unstructured text. Unlike traditional rule-based PII tools that pattern-match formats like phone numbers, Privacy Filter uses deep language understanding to catch contextual and subtle personal data — distinguishing between public information and private identifiers such as names, addresses, emails, account numbers, passwords, and API keys across eight label categories. The model runs locally (meaning sensitive text never leaves the device), processes up to 128,000 tokens of context in a single forward pass using a bidirectional token-classification architecture, and achieves an F1 score of 97.43% on the corrected PII-Masking-300k benchmark. With only 50 million active parameters out of 1.5 billion total, it is fast enough for high-throughput production workflows and can be fine-tuned on small domain-specific datasets — jumping from 54% to 96% F1 in benchmark tests. The model is available now on Hugging Face and GitHub for experimentation, customization, and commercial deployment.

title: Anthropic Launches Claude Opus 4.7 with Major Coding and Vision Upgrades
summary: Anthropic released Claude Opus 4.7, delivering significant gains in agentic software engineering, high-resolution vision, and instruction following while adding new cybersecurity safeguards.
content: Anthropic released Claude Opus 4.7 on April 16, 2026, calling it a notable improvement on Opus 4.6 for advanced software engineering — particularly for the hardest, long-running tasks that previously required close supervision. The model introduces substantially better vision, accepting images up to 2,576 pixels on the long edge (more than three times the resolution of prior Claude models), enabling fine-grained computer-use agents, dense diagram extraction, and pixel-accurate references. In real-world testing, CodeRabbit saw over 10% recall improvement surfacing difficult bugs in complex PRs, Rakuten reported Opus 4.7 resolving 3x more production SWE-Bench tasks than Opus 4.6, and XBOW's visual-acuity benchmark for autonomous penetration testing jumped from 54.5% with Opus 4.6 to 98.5%. Opus 4.7 also introduces a new xhigh effort level in Claude Code (now the default), task budgets for managing token spend across long runs, and the new /ultrareview slash command for thorough code review sessions. As the first model deployed under Anthropic's Project Glasswing cybersecurity initiative, Opus 4.7 ships with safeguards that automatically detect and block high-risk cybersecurity requests, with a new Cyber Verification Program for legitimate security professionals.

title: White House Pushes Federal Preemption of State AI Laws with New Policy Framework
summary: The White House's National Policy Framework for AI signals a major shift toward centralized federal AI governance, proposing to override the patchwork of state-level AI regulations through litigation and legislation.
content: The White House released its National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence on March 24, 2026, building on Executive Order 14365 and marking a notable shift from a hands-off approach to a centralized federal framework for AI governance. The Framework's most consequential element is its push for federal preemption of state AI laws deemed to impose 'undue burdens' on innovation — targeting the growing patchwork of state regulations from Colorado's AI Act to California's automated decision-making rules. To enforce this vision, the Administration proposed an AI Litigation Task Force empowered to identify problematic state laws, assess their constitutionality under the Commerce Clause, and initiate litigation to invalidate conflicting measures. The Framework also calls on Congress to codify these objectives into federal legislation, with Speaker Mike Johnson already signaling support — though bipartisan passage remains uncertain given divided Congress and election-cycle dynamics. Separately, the global regulatory picture is similarly active: Singapore issued new agentic AI governance guidance, South Korea implemented its AI Basic Act, Vietnam's AI Law came into force, and the EU made progress on AI transparency and copyright proposals.

title: Google Launches Deep Research Max for Enterprise-Grade Autonomous Research
summary: Google released Deep Research and Deep Research Max via the Gemini API, offering autonomous research agents capable of blending web searches with proprietary data sources to produce expert-grade, cited analyses.
content: Google launched Deep Research and Deep Research Max on April 21, 2026, built on Gemini 3.1 Pro and available via the Gemini API for developers building research-intensive workflows. Deep Research is optimized for speed and low latency in interactive applications, while Deep Research Max uses extended test-time compute to iteratively reason, search, and refine reports — designed for asynchronous background tasks like nightly due-diligence generation for analyst teams. A key new capability is Model Context Protocol (MCP) support, which lets the agent connect to proprietary data streams such as financial data from FactSet, S&P Global, and PitchBook alongside open-web search, transforming it from a web summarizer into a full autonomous research agent over custom repositories. The agent natively generates charts and infographics inline with reports, supports multimodal inputs (PDFs, CSVs, images, audio, video), and offers collaborative planning so developers can review and guide the research scope before execution. Deep Research Max powers the same infrastructure used in Gemini App, NotebookLM, Google Search, and Google Finance, and is now available in public preview on paid Gemini API tiers.
